articles.headline: Listen Labs raises $69M after viral billboard hiring stunt to scale AI customer interviews
articles.description: Alfred Wahlforss was running out of options. His startup, Listen Labs, needed to hire over 100 engineers, but competing against Mark Zuckerberg's $100 million offers seemed impossible. So he spent $5,000 — a fifth of his marketing budget — on a billboard in San Francisco displaying what looked like gibberish: five strings of random numbers.The numbers were actually AI tokens. Decoded, they led to a coding challenge: build an algorithm to act as a digital bouncer at Berghain, the Berlin nightclub famous for rejecting nearly everyone at the door. Within days, thousands attempted the puzzle. 430 cracked it. Some got hired. The winner flew to Berlin, all expenses paid.That unconventional approach has now attracted $69 million in Series B funding, led by Ribbit Capital with participation from Evantic and existing investors Sequoia Capital, Conviction, and Pear VC. The round values Listen Labs at $500 million and brings its total capital to $100 million. In nine months since launch, the company has grown annualized revenue by 15x to eight figures and conducted over one million AI-powered interviews."When you obsess over customers, everything else follows," Wahlforss said in an interview with VentureBeat. "Teams that use Listen bring the customer into every decision, from marketing to product, and when the customer is delighted, everyone is."Why traditional market research is broken, and what Listen Labs is building to fix itListen's AI researcher finds participants, conducts in-depth interviews, and delivers actionable insights in hours, not weeks. The platform replaces the traditional choice between quantitative surveys — which provide statistical precision but miss nuance—and qualitative interviews, which deliver depth but cannot scale.Wahlforss explained the limitation of existing approaches: "Essentially surveys give you false precision because people end up answering the same question... You can't get the outliers. People are actually not honest on surveys." The alternative, one-on-one human interviews, "gives you a lot of depth. You can ask follow up questions. You can kind of double check if they actually know what they're talking about. And the problem is you can't scale that."The platform works in four steps: users create a study with AI assistance, Listen recruits participants from its global network of 30 million people, an AI moderator conducts in-depth interviews with follow-up questions, and results are packaged into executive-ready reports including key themes, highlight reels, and slide decks.What distinguishes Listen's approach is its use of open-ended video conversations rather than multiple-choice forms. "In a survey, you can kind of guess what you should answer, and you have four options," Wahlforss said. "Oh, they probably want me to buy high income. Let me click on that button versus an open ended response. It just generates much more honesty."The dirty secret of the $140 billion market research industry: rampant fraudListen finds and qualifies the right participants in its global network of 30 million people. But building that panel required confronting what Wahlforss called "one of the most shocking things that we've learned when we entered this industry"—rampant fraud."Essentially, there's a financial transaction involved, which means there...

articles.headline: Nvidia’s spending $4 billion on photonics to stay ahead of the curve in AI
articles.description: Nvidia announced on Monday that it's investing $2 billion each into Lumentum and Coherent, which are both developing photonics technology for data centers, like optical transceivers, circuit switches, and lasers, which are used to move data at high speeds over long distances. Their tech could improve energy efficiency, data transfer speeds, and bandwidth in future […]

articles.headline: I’m on the Meta Oversight Board. We need AI protections now | Suzanne Nossel
articles.description: AI is transforming our world. Accepting independent oversight is the least companies can do to protect our rightsThe speed with which AI is transforming our lives is head-spinning. Unlike previous technological revolutions – radio, nuclear fission or the internet – governments are not leading the way. We know that AI can be dangerous; chatbots advise teens on suicide and may soon be capable of instructing on how to create biological weapons. Yet there is no equivalent to the Federal Drug Administration, testing new models for safety before public release. Unlike in the nuclear industry, companies often don’t have to disclose dangerous breaches or accidents. The tech industry’s lobbying muscle, Washington’s paralyzing polarization, and the sheer complexity of such a potent, fast-moving technology have kept federal regulation at bay. European officials are facing pushback against rules that some claim hobble the continent’s competitiveness. Although several US states are piloting AI laws, they operate in a tentative patchwork and Donald Trump has attempted to render them invalid.Heads of AI platforms like OpenAI’s ChatGPT and Google’s Gemini say they care about safety. But owning the future of AI means pouring billions into models that not even their creators fully understand, and making choices like adding ads – and the capabilities that the Pentagon is now seeking from Anthropic – that raise risk. Anthropic, which styles itself as the most conscientious frontier AI company, says its model is trained to “imagine how a thoughtful senior Anthropic employee” would weigh helpfulness against possible harm. The directive echoes criticisms levied years ago over Silicon Valley companies that shaped the lives of users worldwide from insular boardrooms. Consumers don’t believe they are in good hands. Fully 77% of Americans surveyed last year think AI could pose a threat to humanity. Continue reading...

software_engineering.what_changed: Listen Labs raised $69 million in Series B funding, valuing the company at $500 million, to expand its AI-powered customer interview platform.
software_engineering.why_care: This platform helps product teams gather in-depth user feedback quickly, which can directly inform your development priorities and feature implementations.
software_engineering.what_to_do: Investigate how Listen Labs or similar AI tools could streamline your team's user research process for upcoming features or product iterations.

software_engineering.headline: Nvidia Invests $4 Billion in Photonics for Future AI Data Centers
software_engineering.what_changed: Nvidia is investing $2 billion each into Lumentum and Coherent, totaling $4 billion, to advance photonics technology for data centers.
software_engineering.why_care: These investments aim to improve data transfer speeds, energy efficiency, and bandwidth in data centers, which will directly impact the performance and cost of cloud infrastructure you use.
software_engineering.what_to_do: Stay informed about advancements in optical networking and photonics, as these technologies will shape the next generation of cloud computing infrastructure.

software_engineering.headline: Meta Oversight Board Member Calls for Urgent AI Protections
software_engineering.what_changed: Suzanne Nossel, a Meta Oversight Board member, argues for immediate, independent oversight and regulation of AI to protect user rights and prevent harm.
software_engineering.why_care: As you build and deploy AI-powered features, understanding the ethical and safety implications is crucial to avoid potential legal issues and ensure responsible development.
software_engineering.what_to_do: Review your company's guidelines for AI development and deployment to ensure they address ethical considerations and potential risks, advocating for stronger policies if needed.

real_estate_construction.headline: Listen Labs Secures $69M to Scale AI Customer Interview Platform
real_estate_construction.what_changed: Listen Labs raised $69 million in Series B funding, valuing the company at $500 million, to expand its AI-powered customer interview platform.
real_estate_construction.why_care: This technology offers a faster, more honest way to gather customer feedback, which can help you understand market needs for new developments or improve existing property services.
real_estate_construction.what_to_do: Investigate how AI-powered customer feedback tools could inform your next project or service offering by researching Listen Labs or similar platforms this week.

real_estate_construction.headline: Nvidia Invests $4 Billion in Photonics for AI Data Centers
real_estate_construction.what_changed: Nvidia is investing $4 billion, $2 billion each into Lumentum and Coherent, to advance photonics technology for data centers.
real_estate_construction.why_care: These investments aim to improve data transfer speeds and energy efficiency in data centers, which are critical infrastructure components for many modern real estate and construction operations.
real_estate_construction.what_to_do: Assess your current data infrastructure needs and consider future-proofing your systems with energy-efficient and high-speed data solutions, as these technologies become more widespread.

real_estate_construction.headline: Meta Oversight Board Member Calls for Immediate AI Protections
real_estate_construction.what_changed: Suzanne Nossel, a Meta Oversight Board member, is advocating for immediate, independent oversight and regulation of AI to protect public rights and safety.
real_estate_construction.why_care: The lack of clear AI regulations could create unpredictable risks and liabilities for your business, especially as AI tools become more integrated into design, construction, and property management.
real_estate_construction.what_to_do: Stay informed about proposed AI regulations and consider how potential new laws might impact your use of AI tools in real estate and construction by reviewing industry updates this month.

science_research.headline: Listen Labs Raises $69M to Scale AI-Powered Customer Interviews
science_research.what_changed: Listen Labs secured $69 million in Series B funding, valuing the company at $500 million, to expand its AI platform for in-depth customer interviews.
science_research.why_care: This AI tool offers a scalable way to gather nuanced qualitative data, which can improve how you collect feedback for research, product development, or understanding user needs.
science_research.what_to_do: Investigate how AI-powered interview platforms like Listen Labs could enhance your research by providing deeper insights than traditional surveys or unscalable human interviews.

science_research.headline: Nvidia Invests $4 Billion in Photonics for AI Data Centers
science_research.what_changed: Nvidia is investing $4 billion, split between Lumentum and Coherent, to advance photonics technology for faster and more energy-efficient data transfer in AI data centers.
science_research.why_care: Improved photonics will lead to more powerful and efficient AI infrastructure, directly impacting the speed and scale of your computational research and data analysis.
science_research.what_to_do: Monitor developments in photonics and optical computing to understand how these advancements might influence future AI hardware and your research capabilities.

science_research.headline: Meta Oversight Board Member Calls for Urgent AI Safety Protections
science_research.what_changed: Suzanne Nossel, a Meta Oversight Board member, emphasized the urgent need for independent oversight and safety protections for AI, citing potential dangers like biological weapon instructions.
science_research.why_care: The rapid, unregulated development of AI poses significant ethical and safety risks, which could impact the integrity of your research, data, and the broader scientific community.
science_research.what_to_do: Review your current AI usage and data handling protocols to ensure they align with emerging ethical guidelines and safety best practices, especially when working with sensitive information.
